feed.title: Combined summary - BLISK: Boolean circuit Logic Integrated into the Single Key

entry.title: Combined summary - BLISK: Boolean circuit Logic Integrated into the Single Key
entry.summary: The recent advancement in Bitcoin's authorization policies introduces an innovative method for integrating complex boolean logic into transaction validations, enhancing control over bitcoin spending without sacrificing privacy or efficiency. Traditional Bitcoin mechanisms, such as threshold signatures and scripts, have limitations in expressing intricate conditions due to their simplicity and visibility issues. A new Proof of Concept (PoC) framework has been developed, utilizing a monotone boolean policy over users' long-term keys, compiled into a single signature verification key. This approach simplifies the on-chain component to verifying a singular Schnorr signature, thereby maintaining policy complexity off-chain. The framework's ability to articulate complex policies beyond the capabilities of threshold signatures, like **`(A ∨ B) ∧ (C ∨ D)`**, signifies a significant step forward in transaction policy expressiveness. This novel system employs cryptographic primitives including MuSig2 for AND gates, Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) for OR gates, and Non-Interactive Zero-Knowledge proofs to ensure circuit resolutions' verifiability and integrity. An essential feature is the setup phase that enables non-interactive key rotation while allowing users to continue using their existing long-term keys. This advancement facilitates complex policy enforcement without external visibility of the policy itself, thus preserving privacy and security. For those interested in further exploration, more information can be found in the detailed academic paper at [https://eprint.iacr.org/2026/088.pdf](https://eprint.iacr.org/2026/088.pdf), an explainer blog post at [https://hackmd.io/@olkurbatov/HJm5h0JH-l](https://hackmd.io/@olkurbatov/HJm5h0JH-l), and the framework's codebase at [https://github.com/zero-art-rs/blisk](https://github.com/zero-art-rs/blisk). A query within the correspondence focuses on the operational dynamics of MuSig2 in handling scenarios with multiple signatories, particularly when specific signers are deemed necessary for transaction authorization. The outlined policy allows for a signature generation by four distinct coalitions formed by either A or B with either C or D, illustrating the protocol's flexibility and security. The verification key's formation through ECDH operations between pairs and subsequent aggregation demonstrates a sophisticated blend of cryptographic techniques, ensuring secure and flexible transaction validation. The discussion extends to questioning the necessity and efficiency of employing ECDH and Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKPs) in the context of disjunction subtrees within a Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) policy framework. An alternative suggestion involves Replicated Secret Sharing (RSS) as a simpler method for secure group communication, potentially avoiding the complexity introduced by ECDH and ZKPs. This approach proposes a shift towards a model where secrets are privately distributed among OR-subtree members, aiming to simplify the cryptographic protocol without compromising security. In response, it is clarified that the BLISK approach has several advantages, including avoiding the storage of new secret material and enabling any eligible member to resolve OR-gate issues without full clause co-signature. The method ensures that the shared state among participants cannot be corrupted and emphasizes the importance of ZK proof verifiability, especially during key rotation processes. These features highlight BLISK's focus on maintaining public checkability and non-interactive correctness verification in policy setups and individual key updates, presenting a concrete solution for local-derivability and well-formedness enforcement in cryptocurrency policy frameworks.
